pr.title: Label Kirby Costume pointers, Shared Action States, and fix a bad label
pr.author: PsiLupan

summary: PR 109 is an assembly/data cleanup pass. It turns many raw hex-encoded string blobs and raw pointer constants in asm/data.s into explicit labels, .asciz strings, and label-based pointer table entries, especially around Kirby costume/copy asset data and shared action-state-related data. It also fixes a bad assembly label in asm/melee/lb/code_8001E2F8.s by removing an erroneous lbl_80020000 placed at an instruction address that should not have been a separate global label.

key_files.role: Main cleanup target; adds many labels, converts encoded string data to .asciz, and replaces raw 0x803... pointer constants with label references.
Removes a bad global label, lbl_80020000, from the middle of a sequence of stores.

what_changed: Added explicit global labels for many previously implicit data addresses in asm/data.s, including labels such as lbl_803CAD08, lbl_803CAD20, lbl_803CAF18, lbl_803CB138, and many lbl_803D4Cxx entries.

Converted hex-encoded ASCII .4byte sequences into readable .asciz strings with .balign 4, for example "PlyKirbyPr_Share_joint", "PlyKirbyPr_Share_matanim_joint", and "!(jobj->flags & JOBJ_USE_QUATERNION)".
Replaced raw pointer constants like .4byte 0x803CAD08 and .4byte 0x803CAF18 with symbolic references such as .4byte lbl_803CAD08 and .4byte lbl_803CAF18.
Labeled Kirby costume/copy asset filenames and associated shared joint/matanim strings for multiple color/copy combinations, including Pr, Mt, and Fc groups shown in the diff excerpt.
Added labels for a table of Japanese string data around lbl_803D4C38 through lbl_803D4D64 and updated the table at lbl_803D4D74 to use label references for those entries.
Removed the erroneous .global lbl_80020000 / lbl_80020000 label pair from asm/melee/lb/code_8001E2F8.s.
smart_moves: Kept data addresses stable by introducing address-based lbl_ symbols at the exact locations of recovered strings, then referencing those labels from pointer tables instead of changing layout.
Used .asciz plus .balign 4 to make embedded ASCII readable while preserving alignment expected by the original data layout.
Converted only clearly recoverable ASCII strings in-place, avoiding speculative semantic names where the evidence was only an address label.
Made pointer tables much more searchable and safer for future refactors by replacing hardcoded addresses with assembler-resolved symbol references.
Fixed the bad code label by deleting the label rather than renaming it, indicating the address was not a meaningful branch/data target in the provided context.
decomp_lessons: When a data region contains obvious packed ASCII in .4byte form, converting it to .asciz can improve readability without changing emitted bytes if alignment is preserved.
Pointer tables should prefer label references over raw absolute addresses; this documents table structure and reduces the chance of stale pointer constants during later edits.
Address-based labels are still useful in early decomp when semantic names are not yet known; the immediate win is making data boundaries and pointer targets explicit.
Be careful with labels in hand-written assembly: a spurious global label at an instruction address can imply a false control-flow target and pollute future decomp analysis.
Asset filename patterns can reveal table structure. Here Kirby costume/copy filenames and matching Share_joint / Share_matanim_joint strings appear in repeated color/copy groups.
Readable assertion/debug strings, such as "!(jobj->flags & JOBJ_USE_QUATERNION)", should be represented as strings rather than numeric words when byte layout allows it.
naming_conventions: Recovered strings use original asset-style casing such as "PlKbNrCpPr.dat" and "PlyKirbyPr_Share_joint" rather than inventing normalized C identifiers.
Kirby asset filename pattern visible in the diff: PlKb{Color}Cp{Copy}.dat, with examples Nr, Ye, Bu, Re, Gr, Wh and copy/group suffixes Pr, Mt, Fc.
Shared joint string pattern visible in the diff: PlyKirby{Copy}{Color}_Share_joint, with the base/no-color variant omitting the color code, e.g. "PlyKirbyPr_Share_joint".
Shared material animation string pattern visible in the diff: PlyKirby{Copy}{Color}_Share_matanim_joint.
Unknown or not-yet-semantic data labels remained in address form, e.g. lbl_803CAD08 and lbl_803D4C48, which is appropriate when the PR evidence does not justify a higher-level name.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Replace big-endian ASCII encoded as .4byte words with equivalent .asciz strings.
Follow .asciz strings with .balign 4 to preserve the original 4-byte alignment of subsequent data.
Introduce .global label declarations immediately before recovered string/data objects so pointer tables can reference them.
Change pointer table entries from raw absolute constants to label operands, e.g. .4byte 0x803CB148 becomes .4byte lbl_803CB148.
Remove incorrect labels in code sections when they do not represent real branch targets or meaningful function/data boundaries.
Use repeated asset/table patterns to identify data object boundaries, but keep labels address-based unless stronger semantic evidence exists.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that give semantic names to the Kirby copy/costume pointer tables around lbl_803CAED0, lbl_803CB0F0, and lbl_803CB310.
Search for later decompilation of the lb code around 8001FFF4-80020008 to confirm why lbl_80020000 was invalid.
Look for PRs that migrate these asm/data.s labels into C structs or headers for fighter asset tables.
Search for additional raw .4byte ASCII blocks that can safely become .asciz with .balign 4.
